content:
# Top 5 posts from r/MovieSuggestions day

## 1. What's the most beautiful and emotional film you have ever watched?

**Score:** 67
**Body:** Any suggestions? I want to feel something. I need to watch that will stay in my head for a while. A movie that changed your life in many ways

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Flow. Incredibly beautiful movie. 
2. 
Eternal Sunshine of the Spotless Mind

Cinema Paradiso

Portrait of a Lady on Fire

Life is Beautiful

Coco

Grave of the Fireflies
  - This is scary close to my list

I'd add Up 
  - Eternal sunshine of the spotless mind is one of my all time favorites
3. What Dreams May Come.  Beautiful scenes, but uff... harsh topic.
4. There are so many but I have always loved Lawrence of Arabia. It is truly magnificent.
5. The Straight Story. Beautifully directed by David Lynch & life affirming in so many ways. 
6. A River Runs Through It 
7. Back in the day it was Big Fish that hit me super hard but I haven’t seen it in decades… a more recent one was everything everywhere all at once, I was a mess by the end and had no idea why
8. City of God. I don’t think a lot of the kids were acting. I think it was just life for them and it ripped me apart. 

## 2. Unconventional Documentary recs?

**Score:** 31
**Body:** So my husband and I both love documentaries but on opposite ends. I love true crime and drama/shock factor documentaries and he likes nature ones and the kind that deep dive into communities that you wouldn't see unless your apart of like life on oil rigs or something like Nathan Fielders the rehearsal, but we've found we love insane ones like Renfaire on HBO and right now we're loving the second season of Jury Duty. So any recommendations anyone has for unconventional documentaries I'm open! 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. [Hands On A Hardbody](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hands_on_a_Hardbody:_The_Documentary).

Read as little as possible about it before watching. It’s an absolute masterpiece.
2. I really liked Crumb, and I am not normally a big documentary watcher. Very interesting people.
3. Have you seen tickled?
  - Definitely unconventional.
4. King of Kong: A Fistful of Quarters
5. Grey Gardens


6. The Barkley Marathons is a group of trail runners basically running the most insane marathon up a mountain, looking for books to tear a page out of as proof of completion.

No one won this years. 

Like it’s near impossible, and it was started by a guy named Gary “Lazarus Lake” Cantrell, who is a character.
7. Wild wild country was interesting 
8. Crumb

Exit Through The Gift Shop 


9. Sick: The Life and Death of Supermasochist Bob Flanagan

## 3. looking for obscure 90s gems

**Score:** 36
**Body:** seen over 500 90s films, looking for something a bit more on the obscure/overlooked side.

a few examples i’ve recently watched: One False Move, The Sweet Hereafter, LA Story, Trust, After Dark My Sweet, Wag the Dog, Light Sleeper, The Spanish Prisoner, Fried Green Tomatoes

Thank You in advance for your recommendations

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Run Lola Run
2. Mr. Destiny (1990)

If Looks Could Kill (1991)

No Escape (1996)

Sphere (1998)

Fear and Loathing in Las Vegas (1998)
3. Stepping Out (1991) - About a group of amateur tap dancers putting on a show. 

Defending Your Life (1991) - About the afterlife. Stars Meryl Streep.
4. Arranged from “Must watch at least once” to “If you’re looking to kill an afternoon”, imo. 

Last Night (1998)

Strange Days 

Gummo

Lone Star

The Pentagon Wars

Judgment Night

The River Wild

Screamers

The Late Shift

The Ghost and the Darkness

Unlawful Entry

The Relic

Blankman
5. True Romance (1993) 
  - Obscure?
6. Trust (1990), directed by Hal Hartley
7. Some slightly obscure / hidden 90s gems:

'Happiness' (1999)

'Perdita Durango' (1997)

'Devil in a Blue Dress' (1995)

'The Last Seduction' (1994)

'Dust Devil' (1992)

'The Grifters' (1990)
8. New Year's Day (1989)

200 Cigarettes (1999)

Mystery Men (1999)

Screamers (1995)

Suicide Kings (1997)
9. Not particularly obscure and a little early at 1988 but Working Girl is great. Steel Magnolias, Mystic Pizza. Pretty In Pink. 

## 4. I’m looking for movies about moms that make you cry

**Score:** 21
**Body:** It’s my mom’s birthday today, she died ten years ago and I need to just get into the feels. Any movie movie recommendations with strong mother-daughter bonds, or movies about a sick mom, things like that.

My go to is usually Totoro so if nothing comes thru I will probably end up watching that, but I’m hoping for something new.

Thanks 

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Stepmom
  - Came here to suggest this. That movie makes me ugly cry. 
2. 'Terms of Endearment'. A mother holds your hands for a while, your heart forever.
3. Dumbo 🐘 Gets me every time
4. Steel Magnolias. You will feel all the feels in the most human way. 
5. The Wild Robot
6. I love The Family Stone. 

Sorry for your loss. ❤️
7. The Joyluck Club
8. Petite Maman, All About My Mother, The Long Day Closes, Secret Sunshine, Dark Water, Still Walking, Shoplifters, Mommy, Moonlight, Everything Everywhere All at Once, Saint Omer, Sansho the Bailiff, Earth Mama, Prayers for the Stolen, The Mouth Agape, Cria Cuervos, Letters from Home

Of these, I would extremely highlight Petite Maman, Letters from Home, Sansho the Bailiff, Cria Cuervos, Saint Omer, and Still Walking. I don't think I've ever watched them without crying, and specifically crying about mom shit. 

Petite Maman, The Mouth Agape, and Cria Cuervos are specifically about a mother figure dying (less imminent Shoplifters), so be forewarned 
9. Wild with Reese Witherspoon. 

## 5. James Spader as a Sex Symbol

**Score:** 20
**Body:** Like when James Spader was in Cronenberg’s Crash (1996). Like when he was in Secretary (2002). I don’t know what else he’s been in that would for that category. Gimme hot James Spader pls, preferably when he’s being a little weird like with the preciously mentioned movies.

### Top 10 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Sex, Lies and Videotape.  Sexy just because he listens to women.
  - This is the OG hot Spader movie.
2. White Palace (1990) with him and Susan Sarandon!!
3. As mentioned elsewhere, Sex, Lies and Videotape is the ultimate hot Spader movie.

Spader was drop dead hot in Wolf.

Though more conventionally hot (not as weird) but still gets my motor running, Supernova (2000) is a banger. Him and Angela Bassett melt the screen IMHO.

I have the great good fortune that my hubby looks a lot like Spader.


4. Bad Influence was pretty good
5. Raymon reddington absolutely delightful sex symbol 

İ am hetero dude  btw 
6. Stargate, sexy nerdy Spader
7. Boston legal
8. Pretty in Pink? Although he was a douche in that one
9. Jack's Back (1988)

# Top 5 posts from r/moviecritic day

## 1. Project Hail Mary is going to be Ryan Gosling's biggest solo hit

**Score:** 679

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I‘m a ryan gosling fan since Drive.

My gf suggested this movie. Haven‘t seen a trailer or heard about it.
We went to the cinema. I was blown away.
10/10
2. ![gif](giphy|vP6B55t5F41koebdvO|downsized)

What about the co-star ?
  - Easily the most impressed I’ve been with Dwayne’s range in a movie.
3. Saw it last night.  Fucking excellent film! 
4. If anyone likes the early 2000's era mystery thrillers, I'd highly recommend Fracture.

I'm predisposed to like this movie, so I don't want to over promise, but it's Ryan Gosling as a hotshot DA and Anthony Hopkins as a brilliant aerospace engineer accused of killing his wife.

I watched it when it first came out, and I've been a big fan of Gosling ever since.
  - Fantastic movie.


No idea what it has to do with this post.
5. good movie 👎👎
  - 🤜my🍑
    - Fist my ass?
6. Saw it yesterday. Absolutely fantastic! Will buy the DVD when it comes out for the extra content.  Visually stunning. Will make you laugh and cry. 

## 2. The rage and intensity shown by Hugh Jackman in this scene from Prisoners is phenomenal. From Villeneuve's filmography, which scene has stayed in your mind the longest?

**Score:** 507

### Top 7 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Apparently Hugh wasn't supposed to hit the wall and Paul Danno didn't know he was going to do it. 
  - Heard the same, and Paul's reaction was genuine shock/fear since he wasnt supposed to crumple to the floor afterwards 
2. When Hugh takes Paul Dano between the cars and paul says "they didn't scream". 
  - They didn't cry until I left them


Fucking haunting
    - Thank you for the correction. I should have looked it up first but yeah shit was chilling. 
  - Oh damn we talking about Deni's filmography. I change my pick then. I like the part in Arrival when Amy Adam's goes through the rest of her life. Or sees it I guess. Such rich visual storytelling there. 

Then yeah its the bridge scene in Sicario. All of Sicario really. Is there another movie that has you seeing everything from the wrong protagonists perspective? 


3. Paul Dano loves getting beaten up in his movies 
4. I thought he would have won an Oscar for this performance. It was a gripping display of a desperate father. 
5. Hey mayne... Chill out mayne.
6. For suspense, the Juarez convoy/crossing & home scene in Sicario. 

Some great visual and auditory moments 2049 and Dune. Definitely a treat for the senses.

## 3. What's a movie you enjoyed that almost nobody you know has ever heard of?

**Score:** 440

### Top 9 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. CB4.

I still laugh my ass off when Albert/MC Gusto is getting it on with the hooker on the balcony and all of a sudden he turns into an inflatable for the heavy gyrating. I bring it up with people and I get clueless looks. 
  - I walk around at work with the “I’m Black, Ya’ll” song going on my head. As a middle aged white man, I had to make sure not to sing it out loud.
  - Straight outta Locash! 

2. Robot Jox
3. 8 Heads in a Duffel Bag
4. Ice Pirates
5. Waking Ned Divine 
6. Defendor with Woody Harrelson 
7. "I'm gonna git you sucker" Spoof of blaxplotiation movies, featuring things like the riot games (smash window, grab TV, run a sprint with it) and the hero doing the "all the guns" scene and then turning around and slowly falling over under the weight, and the mentor having his theme music band literally following him around. It's racist, mysogenistic and poking at those tropes in other movies. Sort of a Galaxy Quest treatment. 
8. Hundreds of Beavers

## 4. Should Interstellar Have Also Won The Best Score Oscar?

**Score:** 276
**Body:** I think it 100% should've 

### Top 6 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. It’s one of the greatest scores, ever.  Fits the movie scenes brilliantly.  
2. Well, I have no idea what beat it, but it’s one of the best film scores of the 2010s. 
  - https://preview.redd.it/1ktwl0u33prg1.png?width=591&format=png&auto=webp&s=bf9589b5959c5729bcd63c33bb30c5bab23a4659
    - The Academy probably felt it would be unfair to not give to the guy with 2 noms.
    - Both are totally different scores…

Both are great, too…

And both are Oscar worthy.

    - So yes Interstellar should have won
    - Johan Johansson 😔😞 Such a loss. 
3. Yes
4. It absolutely deserved it
5. Emphatically yes

## 5. What are your honest thoughts on James Gunn's Superman (2025)?

**Score:** 237

### Top 8 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I throughly enjoyed it. Superman is a very human optimist in both personality and heart and film has struggled to portray that. Which was done well here. Lex was also amazing as a genius, billionaire, playboy, philanthropist who’s incapable of enjoying a single moment of his existence always comparing himself to The Man of Tomorrow. I could have done with less Krypto but if he’s gonna be in a movie he was done well too. Very curious where Gunn goes from here the action was done well but the story must come first I’d like an even more personal story and not just another Deadpool and Wolvie buddy cop film with Lex and Clark. Excited for the future of the DCU which I never thought I’d say again.
  - I rewatched for the 2nd time a few days ago. Krypto started getting a little annoying how they used him to get Superman out of every jam.
2. I had a great time watching with friends, and it definitely kept our attention despite being fairly predictable. Does not stick in your mind for long after finishing the movie, which is somewhat to be expected with a superhero “popcorn” flick. 
  - This is a very fair review.

It certainly did not disappoint. It got me excited for more, and more of this new universe.

It didn’t blow me away. The most “let’s fucking go” moment was Mr T. Actually he was the highlight of the movie. (Justice for Darwin!!)
3. I enjoyed it, but there was a lot happening that I assume was groundwork for future projects. I would have liked a more toned--down story with fewer Easter eggs.
  - It’s a shotgun blast that overshoots some of its best ideas. 
4. It was ok. Pretty forgettable. It would be nice to see something new and epic with Superman.
5. It was fun, but messy movie so aight overall, good intro to gunns vision for dc

He’s a great clark kent and superman
6. Loved it, one of the few genuinely enjoyable films I saw in 2025. 
7. Too much happening in a short runtime. The way it started looked like it was a second part and a film preceded it. 

Corenswet was perfectly cast as Superman but superman got beat up too much in the film. Rachel brosnan was good as Lois too. 

The best parts were the justice gang who are actually better than superman himself. 
I wish they used another villain instead of Lex. He has been used too much already

# Top 5 posts from r/TrueFilm day

## 1. Did Murnau change the names in Nosferatu for localization or to avoid copyright issues with the Stoker estate?

**Score:** 5
**Body:** It’s commonly said that Murnau changed the names from the Dracula novel to make Nosferatu and avoid being sued for copyright infringement.

I’ve seen some historians point out though that many original copies of the film had a card near the beginning that credited Dracula. This has lead some to claim that Murnau wasn’t doing the change to dodge lawsuits, but to make the story more palatable for German audiences by using German names.

From the Wikipedia article on Nosferatu: Although those changes are often represented as a defense against [copyright infringement](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Copyright_infringement) accusations,[^(\[3\])](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nosferatu#cite_note-3) the original German [intertitles](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Intertitles) acknowledged *Dracula* as the source. Film historian David Kalat states in his commentary track that since the film was "a low-budget film made by Germans for German audiences... setting it in Germany with German-named characters makes the story more tangible and immediate for German-speaking viewers".[^(\[4\])](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Nosferatu#cite_note-:0-4)

It still seems fishy to me. Like someone who didn’t get permission to use the story and tried to come up with a flimsy loophole.

I thought there might be film historians on here who could clear this up for me and give me a straight answer.

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Yes. On the latter.

The stoker estate sued the filmmakers. 

It's a complex web. Every country has its own copyright laws. And other laws. Hence different versions in different territories.

It was 100% an intellectual property thing. 
2. I don’t know definitively why Murnau changed the names, but that he did it to guard against copyright claims is logical. He did petition the Stoker estate for the rights to adapt the Dracula novel and was denied. Or didn’t ultimately save him from being sued, but it’s a valiant effort.
3. I'm not sure but it's possible that the film may have been deep into production with the hopes that the copyright would be eventually resolved. If that were the case then that would explain why there were the original character names and titles used in early versions.


Then when it became clear that they couldn't work out a deal, the attempt to dodge the copyright came.


Also I wouldn't be sure what Murnau's role was in those changes, if any. He's not a copyright lawyer and I'm not sure if he even had a direct hand in creating whatever text appeared.

## 2. Just watched Gran Torino (2008) for the 3rd time and just now noticed that at Walts confession, he doesn't bring up killing Koreans in the war and only speaks about his family. Why do you think that is? Also why was it snubbbed at the Oscars?

**Score:** 0
**Body:** So I've seen Gran Torino 3 times and it's a good movie to see in different life stages. I saw it when it came out as a kid, in my 20s and in my 30s. I remember even as a kid I saw the ending coming when he gets fitted for a suit, gets a straight shave and does his last confession.

--------

This time I noticed in his confession he confesses kissing a coworker at a Christmas party and not being closer with his sons. The priest responds with "that's it?" and Walt said that all that had been bugging him for 50yrs. Outside of this confession the Father and Walt talked about Walts wartime experiences. Both seem to know Walt killed people in Korea. Walt openly expresses guilt about it both to the priest and to Thau and Walt is ashamed he got the silver star medal for killing people. 

--------

This movie is probably on a top ten list for all time favorite movies and it is my favorite Clint Eastwood movie. So when I looked up it received 0 nominations! I can think of several great jobs by no name actors. Even the bit parts are well acted. What the gang members do in the film is beyond vile but at no point was there any effort to be too heavy handed with how evil they are. Just par for the course for a street gang in a gritty midwestern city.

### Top 5 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I love Gran Torino, but I don't think it was snubbed. In 2009 the Best Picture and Best Director nominees were Slumdog Millionaire, Frost/Nixon, Benjamin Button, Milk, and The Reader. 

Best Actor was Sean Penn for Milk, Richard Jenkins in The Visitor, Frank Langella in Frost/Nixon, Brad Pitt in Benjamin Button, and Mickey Rourke in The Wrestler (which he should have won for imo).

Best Actress was Kate Winslet in The Reader, Anne Hathaway in Rachel Getting Married, Angelina Jolie in Changeling, Melissa Leo in Frozen River, and Meryl Streep in Doubt (my pick for winner).

Best Supporting Actor was Heath Ledger in The Dark Knight, Josh Brolin in Milk, Robert Downey Jr. in Tropic Thunder, Phillip Seymour Hoffman in Doubt, and Michael Shannon in Revolutionary Road.

Best Supporting Actress was Penelope Cruz in Vicky Christina Barcelona, Amy Adams in Doubt, Viola Davis in Doubt, Taraji P. Henson in Benjamin Button, and Marisa Tomei in The Wrestler.

Which of these nominations would you replace with a nomination for Gran Torino? If anything was snubbed for Best Picture it was The Wrestler and Doubt in my opinion, and even then I don't know what I'd drop to make space for them. Maybe Milk at a push, but that's only because I don't personally like biopics all that much, and I still really like Milk. The fact that In Bruges only got one nomination pains me, too, but again I don't know what I'd drop to make room for it.
  - yep, Wrestler was snubbed. 
    - The Wrestler got jobbed. 

The entertainment business is a jealous bitch.
  - Yeah going back to what it was up against I'm less surprised. Thanks.
  - Benjamin Button looks like the weakest of the nominated Best Picture films. Lots of better movies that could have taken its spot. But I agree with you that Gran Torino isn’t one of them. 
2. In addition:

Clint Eastwood intentionally cast a large group of unknown, untrained Hmong American actors for *Gran Torino* (2008), many of whom were recruited directly from Hmong communities in Minnesota, Michigan, and California, rather than through traditional acting channels. 
3. I really enjoy that movie but I’m not surprised or offended by it not getting an Oscar. 

Clint gives a great performance and it’s a very human story, but I don’t think cinematically it’s all too novel and the story isn’t groundbreaking

For me that doesn’t matter, I still enjoy it but the academy had a bias towards novelty because they watch so many movies and they want something that feels different, monumental or subversive (or they get lobbied super heavy lol). A very good generational drama that treads familiar ground is not going to get their attention 
4. Hey! Literally watched the movie for the first time a week or two ago. I would consider myself relatively left leaning in both my social and economic beliefs, but have always had a deep interest in art that argues for something I totally disagree with. This is all lead in to say, I think Clint is easily my favorite conservative, American film maker in his ability to make emotionally persuasive art about topics I think are pretty abhorrent. Although he didn’t write or direct it, I think watching Dirty Harry really blew my mind with how frustrated I found myself getting over a total strawman argument (designed as the perfect piece of persuasion to anyone who thinks cops should have limited power). The rest of his movies similar deal with these sort of conservative perspectives, but he is able to always sprinkle something in that makes you care deeply about these horrible men. Gran Torino is obviously a great example of just how shockingly good he really is as a filmmaker.

Regarding your question, I believe there is a part earlier in the movie where the priest confronts Walt on his porch after a couple failed attempts to convince him to come to confession. Father Janovich essentially explains that he’s worked with other veterans who have taken lives and are haunted by what they were ordered to do, and confession can be very freeing. Walt, still very much opposing him, states something along the lines of “what haunts you isn’t what you were commanded to do, it is the mistakes you make on your own”. 

Later, near the end when Walt decides to confess before the priest, you are absolutely correct that he weirdly confesses to minor acts rather than the much bigger acts of killing he did in the Korean War; the father is confused and asks him if “that’s really all you have to confess?” (maybe expecting him to speak about the killings the Father expects him to go and do, or perhaps the men he killed in Korea). I think the confession is a direct callback to the earlier scene on the porch, and Walt’s lack of need to confess to what he’s about to do or his killings in war speak to a feeling of “doing what has to be done”. It isn’t that he isn’t impacted by taking a life, he explains to Thao that he’s saving him from the haunting feeling of having blood on his hands, but more a reflection of his character’s viewpoint. There is probably more to dig into about the conservatism in the movie, and how Walt sees less of a problem in the disgusting systems he played into than minor “personal” errors that actually felt like his own. At least that’s how I’m reading all of this *shrug*
5. I loved this film also!  I also think it’s an important film.  I saw it (the first time) when I was older than you were the third time you saw it.  But I’ve also seen it several times since and it’s like an awakening every time. I am a huge Clint Eastwood fan of his acting, his directing, and his film choices.  So this film, to me, is just an incredibly well done and well executed movie.  
  - I have always liked no matter how vile what the gangstas did that the film didn't go over the top in trying to make them seem like stylized sociopathic monsters. Yhey reminded me of some people that were notorious in highschool but could still laugh and joke like normal people. The older I get the less I see it as a mixed happy ending and more as a truly happy ending too. Also when I was younger I used to think Thau was a younger kid than he was. Considering he was old enough to drive his girlfriend out on a date and at the end.

## 3. Project Hail Mary (2026) Review - Fun yet forgettable!

**Score:** 0
**Body:** It's a fairly charming film that I enjoyed throughout and was not bored at any point of time. Ryan Gosling did justice to the lead character though the role itself was not among his most demanding ones. Ryan-Rocky pairing was pretty sweet to watch. A couple of shots were very pretty.

But when I ponder the film after walking out of theatre, I can't help but feel that this film is pretty lacking in any interesting themes or sci-fi elements per-say. It presents an interesting sci-fi concept in its first 45 minutes and from there on does nothing but butcher science as to speak. It feels like someone just took Interstellar, Arrival, The Martian and Gravity and came up with a script centred around a warm and funny human and non-human pair, which we have also seen many times in movies like The Iron Giant, Paddington, ET and so on. And those sci-fi movies are great sci-fi movies (barring Gravity, pardon me) because of there core themes which seems missing in this one.

A fun flick, which I will not think about again after a couple of weeks.

### Top 2 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. I saw it with my daughter and we both had a great time. It's not a masterpiece, but not everything has to be. It's just a film that's aiming to be as enjoyable as possible for as many people as possible, and there's nothing wrong with that. SF's a broad church, and it doesn't always have to be deep.

It's not trying to be an intellectual SF film like Arrival or 2001. Instead it's a buddy movie, so it should be judged on whether it succeeds at that. 
  -  View Project Hail Mary movie: [reddit.com/live/1gr03xx3mnx0h](http://reddit.com/live/1gr03xx3mnx0h)
  - I agree, it was a very enjoyable watch!
2. I extremely disagree that it doesn't present any interesting themes or sci-fi aspects. 

I find that its a take done more by people who miss subtext when presented in a more comedic or lighthearted way. 
  - Please enlighten me
  - C’mon let’s here your interesting themes and sci-if aspects. You acted all condescending to OP, made them come up with some themes(when they already said the movie wasn’t all that interesting), and all you did was disagree. Let’s hear your take. 

## 4. Nine & The Intern films treatment of genders

**Score:** 0
**Body:** Would like others opinions on two movies I recently watched. imo Nine is unwatchable. I loved The Phantom Thread but day -Lewis shallow ‘but troubled ‘🤢character in Nine is disgusting to watch. Horrid. That is all….The Intern was written and directed by the esteemed Nancy Meyers. I find it disappointing that Linda Lavin’s character is shunned by De Niro ‘s , as he ultimately becomes a lover to Rene Russo’s character.  the ‘dignified’ aged man starts living with the tall model Russo, ( of course he does ). It would have been utterly refreshing if Ms Meyers could have focused on a woman’s soul and created a connection with Lavins character , instead of being roundly rejected ( I highly suspect due to her deeply wrinkled , less worked on face.) I am a man, 60 yo and can see this shallow cliche in every film- but a film entirely by a esteemed woman. Sad. It reminds me of an unexpected surprise in the pedestrian Netflix movie The Other Missy, where the lead male and I , the viewer , actually fell for the non conventional beauty & her soul , and was turned off by the vapid stunning model. 

### Top 3 comments and their replies (up to depth 10)

1. Re NINE:  Well, he’s a remarkable actor Daniel Day-Lewis but was completely wrong for the lead in Nine.  He completely misunderstood the character.  So I don’t know if that was more of his (mis)interpretation of the character or Rob Marshall’s direction.  

When I was young, I saw the play on Broadway with the incredible Raul Julia and the stunning, voluptuous Anita Morrison.  Both were beyond great - just purring with authentic personal magnetism and charisma.  Just brought the house down!  The character that DDL was interpreting is supposed to be very sexual, very sensual, very charming who is a serial cheater because he can’t keep his hands off women.  It’s a very physical role too.  I was excited to see the movie because I really liked the females in the cast (who I thought were excellent) but DDL just suffered from overthinking the role and exuded zero sex appeal, instead projecting insecurity, no personal magnetism nor charm.  That just was not who the character was.  

And finally in my assessment, it’s a famous musical with incredible, great music and songs and DDL is not even a singer and that was just so obvious and really disappointing if you know the material.

When the Broadway play was revived in the early 2000’s, Antonio Banderas played the lead role of the director, Guido.  He was SO excellent - and had the physical and musical command to be like the larger-than-life character that all these fabulous women would adore and fall for.  He got tremendous reviews for being the “magnetic charmer” that the role requires, and was nominated for a Tony for best actor.  He was seriously considered for the movie role, supposedly, but it ended up going to DDL.  Just total 1) miscasting, and 2) bad direction.  We could imagine if you could get Daniel Day-Lewis for your movie that’s going be your first choice cause he is an incredible actor - probably the greatest of his generation - but it was just miscast with him.
2. I agree with you about *Nine*, a terrible movie by a terribly-commercial director.

I disagree with your interpretation of *The Intern*, which is one of my favourite guilty pleasures - and I also don’t understand why it is held in such low esteem among the cineasts (3.3 on Letterboxd.) This fluffy comedy is not about de Nero relationship with Russo, but with the young CEO, Anne Hathaway. So his romance subplot is not a significant part of the story.

I loved the Linda Levin character from the first time I saw this movie. I thought she played so funny the few lines she was given, and I’ve been seeking her in other movies (none of which were good, unfortunately). But to respond to your particular point about older women presence in movies based on looks, well, there are enough other POVs, especially in “foreign” / non-Hollywood movies. As a 72-yo man, I judge the merits of movie characters not always on their standard looks, but on how good the director is, so it’s mixed. I guess what I’m trying to say is that I’ve moved away from Hollywood fair, because there’s a lot more nuance elsewhere.

3. Not sure where it first originated, other than "John" being a popular mainstream name for men in the US, but the name quickly became a useful shorthand signal to audiences about the character, like a typecast.

5. Because John was an extremely common name for a long time, it became shorthand for "just some guy." In the US, anytime there's a man's body of unknown ID in a system (like a corpse, or a medical patient in a coma with no identifying information), they're called "John Doe." John can signal "nobody in particular" in that way, or it can signal "everyman," i.e. very normal and down to earth.

So when you have an action hero like John McClane, you want to signal that he's normal. He's not Rambo or Batman or some kind of superhero, he's not super buff, he doesn't have crazy secret agent special skills. (Die Hard was somewhat revolutionary for action cinema in its time for this reason.) So you call him John.

When you have an action hero like John Wick, you want to signal how much he is alienated from society, how much his identity has been subsumed into his role as an assassin and his reputation as the Baba Yaga. The whole series starts with the little illusion of regular human life he'd been living in being shattered and he has no other relationships outside his profession. He's probably had multiple names in his life. He's a ghost and he's no one. So you call him John.
